posts.name: Ken Vastola
posts.cooked:
A really interesting article by Ed Zimmerman. I drank that 2009 Le Montrachet with him and Chris Thile in 2015. The potential was obvious.

Forbes

Going Underground: Fontaine-Gagnard & Burgundy's Smallest White Grand...

Fontaine-Gagnard has enviable holdings in all of the legendary Montrachet grand crus in the village of Chassagne-Montrachet. The least well known -- and tiniest white Burgundy Grand Cru -- is Criots-Bâtard-Montrachet, for which Fontaine-Gagnard is...

link_counts.title: Going Underground: Fontaine-Gagnard & Burgundy's Smallest White Grand Cru, Criots-Bâtard-Montrachet

posts.name: Barry P
posts.cooked: Great story, Ken. Thanks for sharing it. I’ve always loved Richard Fontaine’s wines. So crisp, fresh and vibrant.

posts.name: Ken Vastola
posts.cooked:
Barry P:

Great story, Ken. Thanks for sharing it. I’ve always loved Richard Fontaine’s wines. So crisp, fresh and vibrant.

Long time, no see, Barry. We have to rectify that. I am even willing to drink Burgundy.
Always loved this wine - and found the rarity of the vineyard fascinating. Seeing how small Criots is in real life is remarkable.

posts.name: Daniel Bailey
posts.cooked: Think I’ll pop one of my 06 Criots!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: To conclude several thousand words on this particular wine without mentioning the vineyard’s distinctive exposition / topography vis a vis Bâtard-Montrachet, and without adverting to Fontaine-Gagnard’s premox issues (about which Céline is very candid) seems like a bit of an omission!

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
dbailey:

Think I’ll pop one of my 06 Criots!

I will be very interested in your note. My experience with these wines is they taste wonderful young, but do not age well.

posts.name: Chris Bublitz
posts.cooked: I have consumed several bottles of 2012 Fontaine-Gagnard Criots-Batard-Montrachet this year. I thought they were very flavorful and quite delicious. However, to my taste, they seemed fully mature and not for long keeping. I would suggest drinking up in the next year or so.

posts.name: Daniel Bailey
posts.cooked: The first bottle a few weeks ago was explosively powerful and majestic. One of the best white burgs I’ve ever experienced. However, it was clearly doing so with close to its last breath. This bottle is noticeably less mature, more focused and poised but ultimately not quite as thrilling. Still, extremely good though!

posts.name: Michael Bowden
posts.cooked:
Ken V:

I drank that 2009 Le Montrachet with him and Chris Thile in 2009. The potential was obvious.

With just 2-3 months in the barrel? Was it even through fermentation (alcoholic is possible/likely, malo most likely isn’t)?

posts.name: Ken Vastola
posts.cooked:
Michael Bowden:
Ken V:

I drank that 2009 Le Montrachet with him and Chris Thile in 2009. The potential was obvious.

With just 2-3 months in the barrel? Was it even through fermentation (alcoholic is possible/likely, malo most likely isn’t)?

LOL! That’s a typo. It should say in 2015.

posts.name: Kent Comley
posts.cooked:
This is ancient history but I tasted a number of the 2002 F-G soon after release and thought that they were stunning. Purchased CBM, to open it in around 2008 to find it just about shagged.
Methods may have changed since that time, but I would suggest conservative drinking windows be applied.

posts.name: Michael Bowden
posts.cooked: The 04’s were delicious until the got hit by the pre-mox train…

posts.name: Scott Brunson
posts.cooked:
Michael Bowden:

The 04’s were delicious until the got hit by the pre-mox train…

Thankfully I drank mine young, but it pained me to do it.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
Michael Bowden:

The 04’s were delicious until the got hit by the pre-mox train…

Yes!

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: Has anyone actually had a Fontaine Gagnard at age 10 plus that had aged well, or “properly”?

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: due to their premox record, I avoid them like the plague premox is.

Went back through notes and realized I got caught in the twisted Chassagne family tree and loved Blain-Gagnard and their Criots. They had some issues with PremOx, but I was much more lucky here personally.

posts.name: Stephen Williams
posts.cooked: I had their 1982 Montrachet 5 or so years ago which was about as good as White Burgundy gets. A wine from a different era in more ways than one !

posts.name: Matthew Hemming
posts.cooked: De-listed them from work due to unacceptable oxidation issues. Would have bankrupted us to be continually giving credits/replacements for knackered wine and taken half my life hounding people up the supply chain to make us whole again. Just not worth it.
